text: India ink is visibly injected near the tumor site in the sigmoid colon, marking the area for surgical reference.
The surgeon initiates medial retroperitoneal dissection of the mesocolon, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully separate the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon inserts an electric scalpel through the five millimeter port located in the right lower quadrant, preparing for further dissection.
Three ports are equipped with 3.5 millimeter forceps, positioned at the right and left lateral and left lower quadrants.
The surgeon continues the cranial dissection of the mesocolon, advancing the surgical field towards the upper regions of the abdomen.
The surgeon exposes the root of the inferior mesenteric artery and cuts it using an ultrasonic device after applying clips.
The surgeon continues lateral retroperitoneal dissection of the mesocolon, advancing the separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the left ureter and gonadal vessels, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon clips and cuts the inferior mesenteric vein and left colic artery, completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the lateral attachment by dissecting the taut fusion fascia, progressing towards the median dissection plane.
The surgeon dissects the retrolectal space, carefully separating the tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the bilateral hypogastric nerves during the dissection, ensuring these structures remain intact within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the retroperitoneum on both the right and left sides of the upper rectum, carefully separating these areas from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the mesocolon, revealing the colonic walls along the cut line of the distal cone.
The surgeon places an intestinal clip at the oral side of the colonic cut line, securing the tissue in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the intestinal clip using an angle adjuster, and modifies the angle of the N-linear stapler, preparing for precise stapling along the colonic cut line.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a small skin incision at the umbilicus, which measures up to thirty-five millimeters in size.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis using a double stapling technique, joining the two ends of the colon with precision.
The surgeon places a nine-inch French drain into the pelvis through a five-millimeter incision located in the right lower quadrant.
